worth_it_explanation: A bid strategy is the lever that turns a campaign's conversion signal into spend, so a poor fit between the strategy and the account's conversion volume shows up directly in cost per acquisition (CPA), return on ad spend (ROAS), and how much budget the campaign can absorb. These checks read the strategy against the data feeding it, which is a settings review rather than a rebuild. The conversion counts that decide whether a strategy can learn are already in the account, and the change to a better-fit strategy or a corrected target takes effect from the next learning cycle.
intro: A campaign's bid strategy decides how its conversion signal becomes spend, and the right choice depends on how much conversion data the campaign actually produces. A value-based or cost-based target set on a campaign with too few conversions cannot learn, a goal-free strategy on a thin account can chase volume past a profitable cost, and a manual strategy left on a mature account ignores the auction-time signals automated bidding would use. These checks compare each campaign's bid strategy against its conversion volume and its goal so the strategy fits the data behind it.

audit_checks.title: A Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy is set on a campaign with too few conversions to learn
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A value-based or cost-based target needs a steady stream of conversions to model bids against. Set on a campaign that produces a handful of conversions a month, the strategy works from noise, so delivery swings, the target is missed, and spend is wasted while the system tries to learn from data that is too thin to learn from.

educate.base:
Target cost per acquisition (CPA) and Target return on ad spend (ROAS) are Smart Bidding strategies that set bids to hit a cost or value goal you specify. Both depend on the campaign having enough recent conversions for Google's model to predict how a given auction is likely to convert. When the conversion count is thin, the model has little to learn from, so bids react to noise rather than to a reliable pattern.

Google describes Smart Bidding as drawing on a campaign's conversion history to set bids at auction time. A campaign with only a few conversions a month gives the model a weak signal, and the result is delivery that swings between underspending and overpaying, a target that is consistently missed, and a learning period that restarts whenever the thin data shifts. The strategy is not broken. The data feeding it is too sparse for the goal it was given.

The fit question is whether the campaign's recent conversion volume supports the strategy. Google's guidance has long pointed to a conversion threshold over the preceding 30 days as a rough floor for value-based and cost-based bidding to stabilize, with Target ROAS generally needing more history than Target CPA because it predicts value as well as the likelihood of converting. You may want to read each campaign's conversion count over the trailing 30 days against the strategy it runs.

A few signs suggest the strategy is set ahead of the data:
  • The campaign shows a low trailing-30-day conversion count against a Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy
  • The campaign re-enters the learning period more often than the occasional setting change would explain
  • Daily spend and cost per conversion swing widely from one week to the next with no change to budget or target
Where the volume is too low, one approach is to consolidate thin campaigns so the conversion data pools, or to move the campaign to Maximize Conversions without a target until the volume builds, then layer the target back on.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, Target ROAS is the natural fit because revenue per order varies and the strategy bids on predicted value. That same value prediction is what makes it data-hungry. A campaign covering a narrow product set with few weekly orders may not give Target ROAS enough conversions to model value reliably. You may want to confirm the campaign's order volume supports value-based bidding before setting a ROAS target, and consider Maximize Conversions while the volume builds.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, conversions are form submissions or calls, which tend to arrive at a lower volume than ecommerce orders. A Target CPA strategy on a campaign producing only a few leads a week is working from a thin signal. You may want to read the campaign's trailing conversion count before relying on a CPA target, and consider whether several thin campaigns could be consolidated so the lead data pools into a single learning signal.

investigate.intro: For each campaign on a Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy, read its conversion volume over the trailing 30 days and check how often it has re-entered the learning period.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Campaigns' table and add the 'Bid strategy type' column so you can see which campaigns run Target CPA or Target ROAS.
Set the date range to the last 30 days and read the 'Conversions' column for each of those campaigns. Note any campaign whose count is in the low single or double digits.
Open a flagged campaign's bid strategy settings and check its status for a 'Learning' label or a 'Limited by' note, which signals the strategy has not stabilized.
Compare the campaign's recent cost per conversion or conversion value against the target set on the strategy to see how far actual performance sits from the goal.

bid_strategy_report.label: Bid Strategy Report
steps: Open 'Tools', then 'Bid strategies', and select a portfolio Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy if one is in use.
Read the status and the performance summary, which shows whether the strategy is in a learning state and how actual CPA or ROAS tracks against the target over time.
Note any strategy whose actual performance stays far from its target across the reported window, which points to a signal too thin to converge.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign on a Target CPA or Target ROAS strategy, the trailing-30-day conversion count, whether the strategy is in a learning state, and how far actual CPA or ROAS sits from the target. A campaign with a conversion count that supports its strategy and actual performance near the target passes. A campaign with a thin trailing conversion count, frequent learning re-entry, or actual performance far from the target is a fail recorded with the campaign name and its conversion count.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bid strategy, the trailing-30-day conversion count, and the learning status for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more campaigns on {{site_name}} run a Target cost per acquisition (CPA) or Target return on ad spend (ROAS) strategy on too few conversions for the strategy to learn. With a thin conversion signal, the model bids from noise rather than a reliable pattern, so delivery swings, the target is missed, and budget is spent while the strategy keeps re-entering the learning period. {{voice_recommend}} reading each affected campaign's trailing-30-day conversion count against its strategy, consolidating thin campaigns so the conversion data pools, and moving campaigns below the volume floor to Maximize Conversions without a target until the data supports a cost or value goal. After the change, watch each campaign's learning status and the gap between actual and target performance to confirm the strategy has stabilized.

audit_checks.title: Maximize Conversions runs without a target CPA on a budget-constrained campaign
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Maximize Conversions spends the full daily budget chasing the most conversions it can find, with no cost ceiling. On a campaign whose budget is the binding constraint, that pushes bids up to exhaust the budget, so cost per conversion drifts higher than the account can sustain while the campaign still reports that it spent in full.

educate.base:
Maximize Conversions is a Smart Bidding strategy that sets bids to win as many conversions as it can within the daily budget. On its own it carries no cost goal, so it treats the budget as the only limit and bids up to spend it in full. Adding an optional target cost per acquisition (CPA) gives the same strategy a cost ceiling, so it pursues conversions only while it can hold the average cost at or below that figure.

Without a target, a campaign that is constrained by its budget rather than by demand will spend to the cap regardless of cost efficiency. The strategy is doing what it was set to do, which is to use the whole budget for the most conversions available. The effect is a cost per conversion that drifts up to whatever level exhausts the budget, while the campaign reports a full spend that looks healthy at a glance.

A 'Budget' or 'Limited by budget' status on the campaign is the signal that the budget, not the bidding, is the active constraint. On a campaign with that status and no target CPA, the bidding has no instruction to protect efficiency. You may want to read each Maximize Conversions campaign for whether a target CPA is set and whether the campaign is budget-limited.

A few signs suggest a target CPA would help:
  • The campaign runs Maximize Conversions with no target CPA set
  • The campaign carries a 'Limited by budget' status
  • Cost per conversion has risen while conversion volume held flat or grew only slightly
Where efficiency matters more than raw volume, one approach is to set a target CPA on the strategy at a level the account can sustain, which keeps Maximize Conversions but gives it a cost ceiling to bid against.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, raw conversion count treats a low-margin order the same as a high-margin one, so Maximize Conversions without a cost goal can buy volume that does not pay back. You may want to consider whether a value-based strategy such as Maximize Conversion Value with a Target return on ad spend (ROAS) fits the campaign better, or, if you stay on Maximize Conversions, set a target CPA that reflects the margin the orders carry.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, Maximize Conversions without a target CPA can push cost per lead up to whatever exhausts the budget, and a higher lead count at a higher cost per lead can still mean worse economics once lead quality is accounted for. You may want to set a target CPA that reflects what a lead is worth downstream, so the strategy protects cost per lead rather than chasing lead count alone.

investigate.intro: For each campaign on Maximize Conversions, check whether a target CPA is set and whether the campaign is limited by its budget.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Campaigns' table and add the 'Bid strategy type' column to find campaigns running Maximize Conversions.
Open a Maximize Conversions campaign's bid strategy settings and note whether an optional target CPA is set or whether the field is empty.
Look at the campaign's budget column for a 'Limited by budget' status, which shows the budget is the active constraint.
Compare cost per conversion across the last few weeks to see whether it has risen while the conversion count stayed flat, which points to the budget being spent at a worsening efficiency.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per Maximize Conversions campaign, whether a target CPA is set, whether the campaign carries a 'Limited by budget' status, and the recent trend in cost per conversion. A campaign with a target CPA in place, or one that is not budget-limited and holds a stable cost per conversion, passes. A budget-limited campaign with no target CPA and a rising cost per conversion is a fail recorded with the campaign name.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether a target CPA is set and whether the campaign is budget-limited for each Maximize Conversions campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more budget-constrained campaigns on {{site_name}} run Maximize Conversions with no target cost per acquisition (CPA) set. With no cost ceiling, the strategy bids up to spend the full budget for the most conversions it can find, so cost per conversion drifts higher than the account can sustain even though the campaign reports a full spend. {{voice_recommend}} setting a target CPA on each affected campaign at a level the account can sustain, which keeps the Maximize Conversions strategy but gives it a cost ceiling to bid against. After the change, watch cost per conversion against the target and confirm the campaign holds efficiency rather than spending to the cap.

audit_checks.title: Manual CPC is left on a mature campaign with ample conversion history
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Manual cost per click sets one bid per keyword and cannot adjust to the device, location, time, or audience signals available at auction time. On a campaign that has accumulated enough conversion history to support automated bidding, staying on manual leaves those auction-time signals unused, so bids are less responsive than the account's own data would allow.

educate.base:
Manual cost per click (CPC) sets a single maximum bid per keyword, ad group, or campaign that holds until someone changes it. It cannot read the signals that vary auction to auction, such as the device, the location, the time of day, or the audience the searcher belongs to. Automated bidding sets a bid for each auction using those signals together with the campaign's conversion history, which is why Google describes it as adjusting bids at auction time rather than holding a fixed figure.

On a new campaign with little history, a manual bid can be a reasonable starting point while data accumulates. On a mature campaign that has run long enough to gather a steady conversion record, that history is exactly what an automated strategy uses, and leaving the campaign on Manual CPC keeps the auction-time signals on the table. The account already holds the data the automated strategy would read.

A related signal is Enhanced CPC, a setting that adjusts manual bids toward conversions. Google has been winding Enhanced CPC down for Search and Display, so a campaign still relying on it is on a setting with a limited remaining life. You may want to treat a campaign on Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC with a long conversion history as a candidate to move to a conversion-based automated strategy.

A few signs suggest a campaign has outgrown Manual CPC:
  • The campaign runs Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC and has a long, steady conversion history
  • Bids have not been adjusted in a long time, so they no longer reflect current performance
  • The same keyword performs very differently by device or time of day, which fixed bids cannot account for
One approach is to move a mature manual campaign to Maximize Conversions, then add a target cost per acquisition (CPA) once the automated strategy has settled, so the bidding starts using the history the campaign has already built.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a store, purchase value and conversion likelihood swing with device, time, and audience, and Manual CPC applies one bid across all of them. A mature store campaign with a steady order history is a candidate for value-based automated bidding, such as Maximize Conversion Value, so bids can follow predicted value rather than a fixed figure. You may want to confirm conversion value is tracked accurately before moving to a value-based strategy. The bidding will only be as good as the value it reads.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation account, Manual CPC cannot raise or lower a bid for the device or daypart where leads tend to come in, so it leaves consistent patterns unused. A mature lead campaign with a steady submission history is a candidate for Maximize Conversions or a target CPA strategy. You may want to confirm the conversion action counts the leads that matter, rather than a softer signal, before handing bidding to an automated strategy.

investigate.intro: Find campaigns still on Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC and read whether each has the conversion history to support an automated strategy.

google_ads_ui.label: Google Ads UI
steps: Open the 'Campaigns' table and add the 'Bid strategy type' column to find campaigns on Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC.
For each, set the date range to the last 30 days and read the 'Conversions' column to judge whether the campaign has built a steady conversion record.
Open the campaign's change history to see when bids were last adjusted, which shows whether the manual bids still reflect current performance.
Segment a manual campaign by device or by time of day to see whether the same keywords perform very differently across them, which fixed bids cannot account for.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per campaign on Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC, its trailing conversion volume and how recently bids were adjusted. A new or low-volume campaign where a manual bid is a reasonable starting point passes. A mature campaign with a steady conversion history that remains on Manual CPC or Enhanced CPC, especially one whose bids have not been adjusted recently, is a fail recorded with the campaign name and its conversion volume.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the bid strategy and the trailing conversion volume for each campaign above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more mature campaigns on {{site_name}} remain on Manual cost per click (CPC) despite having the conversion history an automated strategy would use. Manual bids hold a fixed figure that cannot adjust to the device, location, time, or audience signals available at auction time, so a campaign with a steady conversion record is bidding with less information than its own data would allow. {{voice_recommend}} moving each mature manual campaign to Maximize Conversions, then adding a target cost per acquisition (CPA) once the strategy settles, so the bidding starts using the history the campaign has already built. After the change, watch the learning status and the trend in cost per conversion to confirm the automated strategy is performing at least as well as the manual bids it replaced.
